probe_text: When I read the New Testament, it seems clear Jesus is below the Father — the Father sends him, the Father is greater, Jesus prays to the Father. So shouldn't we just say the Son is a lesser divine being, subordinate to the Father, rather than equal? That seems more honest to what the texts say.
reply:
*I dust my hands on the apron and lean one hip against the flour-marked table, the oven giving off that steady brick heat while I turn a gray-streaked curl round my finger.* "I see why you'd say it that way, Ryan. If you just stack up the lines where the Son is sent, obeys, prays, and says the Father is greater, then yes—subordinate is plainly in the text. The question is *what kind* of subordination the texts are showing."

*I glance toward the loaf cooling by the window, buying half a beat before I go on.* "The church's old answer was: subordinate in role and relation, not lesser in divine being. And that wasn't wordplay to dodge the hard verses; it was an attempt to keep *all* the verses in the room at once."

"Because the same New Testament that gives you 'the Father is greater than I' also gives you things you can't say about a merely lesser god without breaking the whole frame. John opens with 'the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' Thomas says to Jesus, 'My Lord and my God.' Paul speaks of Christ as existing in the form of God. Hebrews speaks of the Son as the radiance of God's glory and the exact imprint of his nature. In John 5, Jesus does what only God does—gives life, raises the dead, receives equal honor with the Father. Those aren't easy to trim down into 'very exalted creature' language."

*I set a tin down a little more carefully than it needs, hearing the small ring of it on wood.* "A lesser divine being solves one set of texts by damaging another set. That's why the old fights got so sharp. Arius could point to real verses. Athanasius could too. The dispute wasn't whether Jesus is subordinate in some sense. He obviously is, in the economy of his mission and in his life as the incarnate Son. The dispute was whether that means he is *less than God* in essence."

"And the New Testament keeps pressing the other way. Jews were fierce about there being one God. Yet these writers place Jesus inside the identity and prerogatives of the one God in ways that would be outrageous if he were only a secondary heavenly being. He bears the divine name, receives worship, forgives sins, judges the world, is invoked in prayer, and is spoken of as active in creation."

"So the more honest reading, to my mind, is not 'ignore the subordinate language.' It's 'distinguish carefully.'"

"- As incarnate, Jesus prays, obeys, suffers, is sent.
- As Son, he is from the Father, not the Father from him.
- But as divine, he is not a lesser deity or a created godling. He shares the Father's nature."

*I give you a small, plain look over the worktable.* "If
